metadata.subject: اللغة الإنجليزية
metadata.title: Pride and Prejudice & As You Like It

sections.title: Pride and Prejudice - Section 1
sections.definition: Extract from Pride and Prejudice (page 101 of Student's Book) about Mr Bingley's arrival.
sections.explanation: The Bennet family learns about Mr Bingley, a wealthy young man moving to their neighbourhood. Mrs Bennet is excited about the marriage prospects for her daughters.
sections.notes: Mr Bingley earns 4,000-5,000 pounds a year. Mr Bennet enjoys teasing his wife about her eagerness.

examples.problem: Mr Bingley is looking to live in the Bennets' estate.
How much money does Mr Bingley earn yearly?
Why is Mr Bingley's arrival a good thing for the Bennet girls?
examples.solution: He is a potential husband for one of them.

sections.title: Pride and Prejudice - Vocabulary (Section 1)
sections.definition: Key vocabulary from the first extract.
sections.explanation: Understanding words like: fortune, likely, grown up, odd, consider, preference, sociable, mention.
sections.notes: These words help understand character relationships and social context.

examples.problem: a large amount of money =
unfairly treating one person better than others =
friendly =

sections.title: Pride and Prejudice - Section 2 (The Ball)
sections.definition: Extract about the ball where Mr Darcy refuses to dance and insults Elizabeth.
sections.explanation: Mr Bingley dances with Jane twice. Mr Darcy finds Elizabeth 'tolerable but not handsome enough to tempt him'.
sections.notes: Elizabeth finds Mr Darcy's comment amusing rather than insulting.

examples.problem: Why doesn't Mr Darcy want to dance?
examples.solution: He does not like the women in the room.

examples.problem: Who does Mr Darcy think is the only beautiful girl in the room?
How does Elizabeth react to Mr Darcy's comment?

sections.title: Pride and Prejudice - Vocabulary (Section 2)
sections.definition: Key vocabulary from the ball scene.
sections.explanation: Understanding words like: several, introduce, coldly, lively, fancied, fancy, attire.
sections.notes: These words describe social interactions and character attitudes.

examples.problem: help two people meet =
full of energy =
dress =

sections.title: Pride and Prejudice - Section 3 (Netherfield)
sections.definition: Extract where Elizabeth visits her sick sister Jane at Netherfield.
sections.explanation: The Bingley sisters criticize Elizabeth's appearance and social connections. Mr Darcy compliments her eyes.
sections.notes: Caroline Bingley is jealous of Mr Darcy's attention to Elizabeth.

examples.problem: Why does Elizabeth only like Mr Bingley?
examples.solution: Because he is friendly and kind.

examples.problem: Why were Bingley's sisters worried about Elizabeth's appearance?
examples.solution: They thought she looked messy after walking alone.

examples.problem: What does Louisa mean by 'such a father and mother'?
examples.solution: She is criticizing the Bennets' lower social class.

sections.title: Pride and Prejudice - Vocabulary (Section 3)
sections.definition: Key vocabulary from the Netherfield scene.
sections.explanation: Understanding words like: afraid, lacking, untidy, indifferent, excessively.
sections.notes: The Bingley sisters show their snobbery through their language.

examples.problem: fear =
messy =
not caring much =

sections.title: Pride and Prejudice - Section 4 (Mr Wickham)
sections.definition: Extract where Mr Wickham tells Elizabeth about Mr Darcy's cruelty.
sections.explanation: Mr Wickham claims Mr Darcy denied him the inheritance promised by his father. Elizabeth believes Wickham's story.
sections.notes: This creates Elizabeth's prejudice against Mr Darcy.

examples.problem: Why was Mr Wickham not able to look for legal advice?
examples.solution: He was too young and had no money.

examples.problem: Why doesn't Mr Wickham disgrace Mr Darcy publicly?
examples.solution: Because of his respect for Mr Darcy's father.

examples.problem: What does Elizabeth think of Mr Wickham?
examples.solution: She thinks he is a good man who was treated unfairly.

sections.title: Pride and Prejudice - Vocabulary (Section 4)
sections.definition: Key vocabulary from Mr Wickham's story.
sections.explanation: Understanding words like: attached, inheritance, accuse, injustice, irritate, malicious, regarded.
sections.notes: These words reveal the conflict between Darcy and Wickham.

examples.problem: very close to someone =
blame someone of something =
evil =

sections.title: Pride and Prejudice - Section 5 (Mr Darcy's Proposal)
sections.definition: Extract where Mr Darcy proposes to Elizabeth and she rejects him.
sections.explanation: Elizabeth accuses Darcy of ruining Jane's happiness and treating Wickham cruelly. Darcy admits he separated Bingley from Jane.
sections.notes: Elizabeth's rejection is strong and emotional.

examples.problem: Why does Elizabeth dislike Mr Darcy so strongly?
examples.solution: Because of everything he said and she heard about him.

examples.problem: Under what circumstances would Elizabeth have accepted?
examples.solution: There was nothing he could have done.

examples.problem: What does Mr Darcy wish at the end?
examples.solution: He wishes Elizabeth could understand his feelings.

sections.title: Pride and Prejudice - Vocabulary (Section 5)
sections.definition: Key vocabulary from the proposal scene.
sections.explanation: Understanding words like: detest, reveal, faults, prevented, endeavouring, tone.
sections.notes: This vocabulary expresses strong emotions and criticism.

examples.problem: hate =
mistakes, a bad part of someone's character =
the way someone speaks =

sections.title: Pride and Prejudice - Section 6 (The Reconciliation)
sections.definition: Extract where Elizabeth and Darcy reconcile and get engaged.
sections.explanation: Darcy admits his faults. Elizabeth admits her feelings have changed. Lady Catherine's opposition made Darcy hope.
sections.notes: Darcy's letter about Wickham and his help with Lydia changed Elizabeth's opinion.

examples.problem: What does Mr Darcy mean by 'you are too generous to play with me'?
examples.solution: He believes Elizabeth would not joke about something so serious.

examples.problem: Why did Lady Catherine's visit make Mr Darcy hope?
examples.solution: Because Elizabeth stood up to her, showing she might care for him.

examples.problem: Who does Elizabeth feel is most to blame?
examples.solution: She blames herself and Darcy equally.

sections.title: Pride and Prejudice - Vocabulary (Section 6)
sections.definition: Key vocabulary from the reconciliation scene.
sections.explanation: Understanding words like: generous, frankly, period, valuable, unsuccessfully, argue.
sections.notes: This vocabulary reflects emotional maturity and resolution.

examples.problem: nice, kind =
clearly =
discuss, fight =

sections.title: As You Like It - Section 1
sections.definition: Extract from As You Like It (page 115 of Student's Book) about Orlando and Oliver.
sections.explanation: Orlando complains about his brother Oliver's mistreatment. Oliver plots to have Charles the wrestler hurt Orlando.
sections.notes: Orlando is the younger brother. Oliver is the eldest who inherited everything.

examples.problem: What does Orlando think of his brother calling him a villain?
examples.solution: He thinks it's unfair because he has done nothing wrong.

examples.problem: What is Oliver's plan to get rid of Orlando?
examples.solution: To have Charles the wrestler hurt him in the fight.

examples.problem: Why did Sir Frederick allow Rosalind to remain at court?
examples.solution: Because she is friends with his daughter Celia.

sections.title: As You Like It - Old English Vocabulary
sections.definition: Understanding old English words from Shakespeare's original text.
sections.explanation: Shakespeare uses words like: wilt, thee, thrice, thy, fool, disliked.
sections.notes: These words are archaic but common in Shakespeare's plays.

examples.problem: will =
you =
your/yours =

sections.title: As You Like It - Section 2 (The Escape)
sections.definition: Extract about Rosalind, Celia, and Touchstone escaping to the forest.
sections.explanation: Hisperia tells the court that Celia and Rosalind have escaped. Frederick orders Oliver to find his brother Orlando.
sections.notes: Rosalind and Celia disguise themselves as men: Ganymede and Aliena.

examples.problem: Who is also missing besides Celia and Rosalind?
What does Hisperia believe?
examples.solution: That Celia and Rosalind are with Orlando.

examples.problem: Frederick orders Oliver to:

sections.title: As You Like It - Vocabulary (Section 2)
sections.definition: Understanding old English words from this section.
sections.explanation: Words like: gentlewoman, overheard, oft, remember, didst, hast, thine.
sections.notes: These archaic forms add authenticity to Shakespeare's dialogue.

examples.problem: a lady =
overhead =
your =

sections.title: As You Like It - Section 3 (Orlando's Poems)
sections.definition: Extract where Orlando hangs poems about Rosalind on trees.
sections.explanation: Orlando writes love poems for Rosalind and hangs them on trees. He meets Ganymede (Rosalind in disguise) who offers to cure him of love.
sections.notes: Rosalind, disguised as Ganymede, pretends to be a man to help Orlando.

examples.problem: Why does Orlando hang the poems on the trees?
examples.solution: To express his love for Rosalind publicly.

examples.problem: What is Ganymede's request to Orlando?
examples.solution: To court Ganymede as if he were Rosalind.

examples.problem: Where do Rosalind and Orlando go?

sections.title: As You Like It - Section 4 (Phoebe and Silvius)
sections.definition: Extract where Phoebe falls in love with Ganymede.
sections.explanation: Silvius loves Phoebe, but Phoebe loves Ganymede (Rosalind in disguise). Rosalind tries to help Silvius and tells Phoebe to love him instead.
sections.notes: This creates a love triangle that will be resolved at the end.

examples.problem: Why does Phoebe run away from Silvius?
examples.solution: She does not return his love.

examples.problem: Why does Phoebe fall in love with Ganymede?
examples.solution: Because Ganymede scolded her, which she found attractive.

examples.problem: What does Ganymede tell Phoebe?

sections.title: As You Like It - Section 5 (Orlando Saves Oliver)
sections.definition: Extract where Orlando saves Oliver from a lioness.
sections.explanation: Orlando rescues Oliver from a lioness, and Oliver becomes a changed man. They reconcile. When Rosalind hears Orlando was wounded, she faints.
sections.notes: Oliver falls in love with Celia after this event.

examples.problem: Why did Orlando save Oliver?
examples.solution: Because he was a kind person.

examples.problem: Why is Oliver a changed man?
Why did Rosalind faint?
examples.solution: Because of Oliver's story about Orlando being wounded.

sections.title: As You Like It - Section 6 (The Wedding)
sections.definition: Final extract where all couples are married.
sections.explanation: Hymen (god of marriage) appears to marry the couples: Rosalind and Orlando, Celia and Oliver, Phoebe and Silvius, Touchstone and Audrey. Duke Frederick becomes a religious man.
sections.notes: All conflicts are resolved, and everyone gets married.

examples.problem: What does Hymen tell the couples?
examples.solution: He marries them and blesses the unions.

examples.problem: Who does Phoebe marry?
What did Duke Frederick do?
examples.solution: He became a religious man and gave up his crown.
